admirablePeople.title: People You Admire
admirablePeople.description: List 3–5 people you admire. They can be people you know personally, public figures, or historical figures. For each, note the qualities you find admirable.

coreValues.title: Your Core Values
coreValues.description: Look at the qualities you listed above. What patterns do you notice? Distill them into your core values — the principles that drive your decisions.
coreValues.reflectionPlaceholder: Any thoughts about what you've discovered...

stepSubtitles.intro: A quick check-in across the areas that shape your life
stepSubtitles.rate: How fulfilled are you in each area?
stepSubtitles.focus: Pick 2–3 areas you want to improve this year
stepSubtitles.reflectReflection: What has this year taught you?
stepSubtitles.reflectStandalone: What insights does your wheel reveal?

intro.snapshotDescription: The circle represents your whole life. Each slice is a life area that matters to you. You will rate how aligned each area feels with your values today.
intro.instruction1: Rate each area from 1–10 (10 = most aligned).
intro.instruction2: Notice where life feels out of balance or out of sync.
intro.instruction3: There is no perfect wheel — the goal is insight, not a "10" everywhere.
intro.changeSlicesHint: Want to change the slices? Update your Life Areas and come back anytime.
intro.startingShapeCaption: This is your starting shape before you rate.

rater.highlightCaption: Highlighted slice is the area you're rating.
rater.alignmentQuestion: How aligned does this feel with your values today?

emotions.label: What emotions do you feel when assessing this area?
emotions.placeholder: Name the emotions that come up...

uplift.label: What is currently pulling this score up?
uplift.placeholder: What is working well right now...

drag.label: What is currently pulling this score down?

change.label: What would need to change in your life to improve this score?
change.placeholder: Small or big shifts that would help...

control.label: How much of this score feels within your control right now?
control.placeholder: What is within your control vs. outside it...

focusPicker.description: Review the areas that stand out in your wheel before continuing.
focusPicker.validationMessage: Select at least 1 area to continue

reflectionStep.description: Take a moment to reflect on what your wheel reveals.
reflectionStep.overallNotesPlaceholder: Any overall thoughts about your wheel of life...

reflectionPrompts.growth: Where did you grow the most this year?
reflectionPrompts.surprise: What surprised you about your scores?
reflectionPrompts.gratitude: Which area are you most grateful for?
reflectionPrompts.overallBalance: How are you satisfied with the overall balance?
reflectionPrompts.negativeImpact: Which areas negatively impact other areas?
reflectionPrompts.positiveImpact: Which areas if improved would improve other areas?
reflectionPrompts.energyCare: Are you investing energy where you actually care?

domainSelector.description: Choose the areas of life you'd like to assess. You can add, remove, or rename any area.
domainSelector.validationMessage: Add at least 3 areas to continue

comparison.attentionHint: This area might benefit from attention

comingSoon.title: Coming Soon
comingSoon.description: This exercise is not yet available.

shadowBeliefs.title: Shadow Beliefs
shadowBeliefs.description: Shadow beliefs are deep, often unexamined assumptions that can sabotage your behavior. This exercise helps you surface them — not to judge, but to build awareness.

beliefs.title: Which beliefs resonate with you?
beliefs.description: Select any that feel familiar, or add your own.

commonBeliefs.somethingWrong: There is something wrong with me
commonBeliefs.dontDeserve: I don't deserve what I want
commonBeliefs.doEverythingMyself: I have to do everything myself

advice.title: Advice You Give Others
advice.description: What advice do you frequently give to others? Do you follow it yourself?

reframe.title: Reframe Your Beliefs
reframe.description: For the beliefs you identified, try writing a more constructive version.

shadowBeliefs.reflectionPlaceholder: Any thoughts about what you've noticed...

curiosities.title: What Are You Curious About?
curiosities.description: List five things you're genuinely curious about. Don't filter — include anything that draws your attention.

intersection.title: The Intersection
intersection.description: Look at your five curiosities. What connects them? What's at the intersection?
intersection.placeholder: What connects all five? What theme emerges?

problems.title: Big Problems
problems.description: Around this intersection, what are five big problems you'd love to help solve?
problems.placeholder: A problem you want to solve

purpose.title: Your Purpose Statement
purpose.description: Write a one-sentence purpose statement. This is provisional — try it for 2–3 months and expect it to evolve.

intro.title: Thought Record
intro.description: A thought record helps you examine and restructure negative thinking patterns. You'll work through a structured process based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT).
intro.howItWorksDescription: By examining the evidence for and against your automatic thoughts, you can develop more balanced perspectives and reduce emotional distress.

situation.title: Describe the situation
situation.description: What happened? What would a camera have recorded?
situation.placeholder: Describe the situation as factually as possible...

emotions.title: How are you feeling about this situation?
emotions.description: Select emotions and rate their intensity.

thoughts.title: What thoughts went through your mind?
thoughts.description: List the automatic thoughts you had. Mark the most emotionally charged one as the "hot thought."

evidenceFor.title: What evidence supports this thought?
evidenceFor.description: What facts or experiences support this thought being true?

evidenceAgainst.title: What evidence goes against this thought?
evidenceAgainst.description: What facts, experiences, or alternative explanations go against this thought?
evidenceAgainst.followUpPlaceholder: Nothing comes to mind... / Tell me more...

balancedThought.title: Write a balanced thought
balancedThought.description: Considering all the evidence, write a more balanced thought.
balancedThought.placeholder: A more balanced way to think about this...
balancedThought.followUpPlaceholder: Can you try a different angle?

rerate.title: Re-rate your emotions now
rerate.description: After examining the evidence and developing a balanced thought, how do you feel now?

learningIntro.title: What Are Cognitive Distortions?
learningIntro.description1: Cognitive distortions are automatic thinking patterns that distort reality. Everyone has them. Learning to recognise them is the first step to thinking more clearly.
learningIntro.description2: In this exercise you will learn 14 common distortions identified by David Burns, then check which ones you recognise in your own thinking.

selfCheck.title: Self-Check
selfCheck.description: Which of these do you recognise in your own thinking? Tap to select.
selfCheck.personalReflectionsDescription: Optionally, describe a personal example for each distortion you recognised.

appliedThought.title: Examine a Thought
appliedThought.description: Enter a thought you would like to check for cognitive distortions.
appliedThought.placeholder: What thought would you like to examine?

appliedDistortions.title: Spot the Distortions
appliedDistortions.description: Which distortions do you think apply to this thought?
appliedDistortions.explanationPlaceholder: Why does this distortion apply to your thought?

appliedResults.title: Results
appliedResults.reframePlaceholder: How could you think about this differently?
appliedResults.notesPlaceholder: Any other thoughts about this analysis...

capture.title: What's worrying you?
capture.description: Describe the worry in your own words. Getting it out of your head and onto the screen is the first step.
capture.emotionsDescription: Pick the emotion that best captures how this worry makes you feel.

classify.title: What kind of worry is this?
classify.description: Understanding the type of worry helps you respond to it effectively.
classify.realProblemDescription: Something concrete you can actually do something about
classify.hypotheticalDescription: A "what if" scenario that may never happen

canAct.title: Can you take action right now?
canAct.description: Is there a concrete step you can take on this problem today?
canAct.yesDescription: There is something concrete I can do about this today
canAct.noDescription: I need to wait or schedule this for later

actionPlan.title: Plan Your Action
actionPlan.description: What is the very next concrete step you can take? Keep it small and specific.
actionPlan.createCommitment: Create a Commitment from this action

schedule.title: Schedule It
schedule.description: Even though you can't act right now, you can plan for when you will. Write down what you'll do and when.
schedule.placeholder: When the time is right, I will...
schedule.encouragement: You've acknowledged the problem and scheduled a time to deal with it. For now, take a deep breath. You don't need to solve this in this moment.

lettingGo.title: Letting Go
lettingGo.description: This worry is about something that hasn't happened and may never happen. That's completely normal. Our minds are wired to anticipate threats, even imaginary ones.
lettingGo.choosePrompt: Choose a technique that resonates with you right now:

leavesOnStream.name: Leaves on a Stream
leavesOnStream.description: Visualize placing the worry on a leaf and watching it float gently down a stream, away from you.

refocus.name: Refocus
refocus.description: Gently redirect your attention to what you are doing right now. Notice five things you can see around you.

worryTime.name: Worry Time
worryTime.description: Note this worry for a scheduled 15-minute worry window later today. You have permission to set it aside until then.

acceptance.name: Acceptance
acceptance.description: Acknowledge the uncertainty without fighting it. You don't have to resolve every unknown right now.

recheck.title: How are you feeling now?
recheck.description: Take a moment to check in with yourself. Rate the same emotion again.

intro.title: Core Beliefs Exploration
intro.description: Core beliefs are the deepest thoughts we hold about ourselves, others, and the world. They form early in life and shape how we interpret everything that happens to us.
intro.techniqueDescription: By repeatedly asking "If that were true, what would it mean?", you can trace a surface thought down to the deeper belief underneath it.

startingThought.title: What thought do you want to explore?
startingThought.description: Think of a recent thought that kept coming back. It could be something triggered by a conversation, a mistake, or a situation that bothered you.
startingThought.placeholder: e.g., 'I always mess things up' or 'Nobody really cares about me'

downwardArrow.title: Downward Arrow
downwardArrow.description: For each answer, ask: "If that were true, what would it mean about you, others, or the world?"
downwardArrow.questionLabel: If that were true, what would it mean?

coreBelief.title: Your Core Belief
coreBelief.description: Based on your exploration, this appears to be the core belief underneath. You can edit it.

self.label: About Myself
self.description: "I am unlovable", "I am incompetent", "I am worthless"

others.label: About Others
others.description: "People can't be trusted", "Others will always leave"

coreBelief.believabilityTitle: How much do you believe this right now?

evidenceFor.title: Evidence supporting this belief
evidenceFor.description: What experiences or facts make you believe this is true?

evidenceAgainst.title: Evidence against this belief
evidenceAgainst.description: What experiences, facts, or alternative explanations go against this belief?

alternative.title: Write a balanced alternative belief
alternative.description: Considering the evidence on both sides, write a more balanced, realistic belief.
alternative.placeholder: A more balanced way to see this...
alternative.followUpPlaceholder: Can you try a different angle?

intro.title: Compassionate Letter
intro.description: Self-compassion means treating yourself with the same kindness you'd show a good friend. When we struggle, we often become our own harshest critic. This exercise helps you respond to difficult moments with warmth instead of judgment.
intro.selfKindnessDescription: Being warm toward yourself when you suffer, rather than ignoring your pain or punishing yourself.
intro.commonHumanityDescription: Recognizing that suffering and personal inadequacy are part of the shared human experience.
intro.mindfulnessDescription: Holding painful thoughts and feelings in balanced awareness, rather than over-identifying with them.

situation.title: What happened, or what are you struggling with?
situation.description: Describe it as if telling a trusted friend. There's no need to analyze or judge — just say what's going on.
situation.placeholder: Something happened that's been weighing on me...

emotions.title: How are you feeling?
emotions.description: Select the emotions that come up when you think about this situation.

critic.title: What is your inner critic saying?
critic.description: Write down the harsh things you're telling yourself. Getting them out makes them easier to examine.

response.title: Write your compassionate response
response.description: Write as if you were a caring friend who knows everything about you and loves you unconditionally. What would they say?
response.placeholder: Dear me, I want you to know that...
response.llmHint: Use this as inspiration — the letter should be in your own words:

takeaways.title: What resonated with you?
takeaways.description: Distill the key messages you want to carry with you. What will you remember from this letter?

intro.title: Positive Data Log
intro.description: The Positive Data Log helps you systematically collect evidence that contradicts a negative belief. Over time, this shifts the belief from "always true" to "sometimes true."
intro.step1: Create a target belief you want to challenge
intro.step2: Log evidence daily that contradicts it
intro.step3: Watch the belief weaken over time

targetBelief.title: What negative belief do you want to challenge?
targetBelief.description: This should be a core belief like "I am not good enough" or "People can't be trusted." If you've completed the Core Beliefs exploration, you can use the belief you identified there.
targetBelief.placeholder: e.g., 'I am unlovable' or 'I always fail at important things'
targetBelief.believabilityTitle: How much do you believe this right now?

confirmation.title: Ready to Start Logging
confirmation.description: Once created, you can return to this log daily to add evidence that contradicts the belief. Over time, you'll see the belief weaken.

log.addEvidenceDescription: What happened today that contradicts your belief?
log.evidencePlaceholder: What happened that contradicts this belief?
log.rerateDescription: Considering the evidence you've collected, how much do you believe it now?
log.noEvidenceYet: No evidence logged yet. Start by adding your first entry above.

intro.title: Behavioral Experiment
intro.description: A behavioral experiment tests whether a belief you hold is actually true. Instead of arguing with the thought, you design a real-world test and observe what happens.
intro.howItWorksDescription: By comparing your prediction with what actually happens, you build a more accurate picture of reality and reduce the grip of unhelpful beliefs.

belief.title: What belief do you want to test?
belief.description: Write the belief as clearly as you can. It should be something testable through action, not just a feeling.
belief.placeholder: e.g., 'If I speak up in the meeting, everyone will think I'm stupid.'
belief.believabilityLabel: How strongly do you believe this right now? {value}%

prediction.title: What do you predict will happen?
prediction.description: Based on the belief, what specific outcome do you expect? The more concrete, the easier it is to compare with reality afterwards.
prediction.placeholder: e.g., 'People will roll their eyes or interrupt me within 10 seconds.'
prediction.confidenceLabel: How confident are you in this prediction? {value}%

design.title: Design your experiment
design.description: What will you actually do to test this prediction? Keep it specific, achievable, and low-risk. Think of it as a curious scientist running a small test.
design.placeholder: e.g., 'At tomorrow's team standup, I'll share one idea about the current project.'
design.whenLabel: When will you run this experiment? (optional)

safety.title: Safety behaviors to watch for
safety.description: Safety behaviors are subtle avoidance patterns that could invalidate your experiment. For example, if you plan to speak up in a meeting but only do it when the boss is absent, the experiment isn't a fair test. List any behaviors you might default to.
safety.placeholder: e.g., 'Only speaking when I'm 100% sure of the answer'
safety.saveChoiceDescription: You can save this experiment as planned and come back later to record the outcome, or record the outcome right now.

outcome.title: What actually happened?
outcome.description: Describe the outcome as factually as you can. What did you observe? How did people respond? What surprised you?
outcome.learnedDescription: How does the outcome compare to your prediction? What does this tell you about the original belief?
outcome.learnedPlaceholder: The key thing I learned is...
outcome.rerateLabel: How strongly do you believe this now? {value}%

intro.title: Behavioral Activation
intro.description: When mood is low, we tend to withdraw from activities that once brought us joy or a sense of achievement. Behavioral Activation reverses this cycle by scheduling pleasant and meaningful activities, then tracking how they affect your mood.
intro.step1: Rate your current mood as a baseline
intro.step2: Plan at least 3 activities across different categories
intro.step3: Track your mood before and after each activity

moodBaseline.title: How are you feeling right now?
moodBaseline.description: Rate your overall mood on a scale of 0 to 10. This gives you a baseline to compare against at the end of the week.
moodBaseline.weekStartDescription: Which week are you planning activities for?

planActivities.title: Plan your activities
planActivities.description: Add at least 3 activities for the week. Mix different categories for the best effect.
planActivities.activityNamePlaceholder: e.g., Walk in the park, Call a friend...

summary.title: Your Week Plan
summary.trackDescription: Optionally mark activities as completed and rate your mood before and after each one. You can also save the plan now and track later.
summary.notesPlaceholder: Any reflections or observations about this week...

intro.title: Structured Problem Solving
intro.description: When problems feel overwhelming, breaking them into steps makes them manageable. This exercise uses a systematic approach from problem-solving therapy (D'Zurilla & Nezu) to help you define, brainstorm, evaluate, and act.
intro.howItWorksDescription: You'll generate multiple solutions without judgment, then evaluate each one to find the best path forward.

problem.title: Define the problem
problem.description: Be as specific as possible. A well-defined problem is halfway solved.
problem.placeholder: Describe the problem clearly and specifically...
problem.emotionsTitle: How are you feeling about this?
problem.emotionsDescription: Select the emotions this problem brings up for you.

brainstorm.title: Brainstorm solutions
brainstorm.description: Generate as many ideas as you can. Quantity over quality — don't judge yet. Include creative and unconventional ideas too.

evaluate.title: Evaluate your solutions
evaluate.description: For each solution, list pros and cons, then rate feasibility and effectiveness.
evaluate.effectivenessDescription: How well would it solve the problem?

choose.title: Choose your best solution
choose.description: Solutions are ranked by their average of feasibility and effectiveness. Select the one you want to commit to.
choose.actionPlanDescription: How will you implement this solution? Be specific and concrete.

review.title: Review Your Plan
review.markCompletedDescription: If you've already resolved this problem, you can record the outcome now. Otherwise, leave it as in-progress and come back later.
review.outcomePlaceholder: What was the outcome? What happened?
review.emotionsAfterDescription: Re-check your emotions after working through this problem.

intro.title: Graded Exposure Hierarchy
intro.description: Like climbing a ladder — you start with the easiest step and work your way up. By gradually facing feared situations, your anxiety naturally decreases over time.
intro.ladderDescription: You'll build a list of situations rated by how much distress they cause (0–100), then work through them from easiest to hardest.

fearTarget.fearDescription: Describe the fear, phobia, or anxiety you want to work on.
fearTarget.fearPlaceholder: e.g., Public speaking, spiders, social situations...
fearTarget.goalDescription: What would you like to be able to do when you've worked through this fear?
fearTarget.goalPlaceholder: e.g., Give a presentation at work confidently

buildHierarchy.title: Build your fear ladder
buildHierarchy.description: Add situations related to your fear, each with a distress rating (SUDS: 0–100).
buildHierarchy.tipDescription: Aim for 8–15 items spanning the full range (0–100). Include some easy ones (20–30) to build confidence.

safetyBehaviors.title: Safety behaviors to watch for
safetyBehaviors.description: Safety behaviors are things you do to reduce anxiety in the moment — but they can prevent you from learning that you can cope. Identifying them helps you do exposures more effectively.
safetyBehaviors.placeholder: e.g., Avoiding eye contact, checking phone...

intro.title: Three Pathways to Meaning
intro.description: Viktor Frankl identified three ways we discover meaning in life. This exercise helps you explore each pathway and find your unique meaning balance.
intro.creativeDescription: What you give to the world through work, creation, and deeds.
intro.experientialDescription: What you receive from the world through love, beauty, and truth.
intro.attitudinalDescription: The stance you choose toward unavoidable suffering.
intro.quote: Life can be made meaningful in a threefold way: first, through what we give to life (creative values); second, by what we take from the world (experiential values); third, through the stand we take toward a fate we no longer can change (attitudinal values).
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

creative.title: Creative Values
creative.description: What do you give to the world? Think about your work, projects, skills, contributions, and anything you create or build.
creative.placeholder: e.g., Teaching others what I know, building software that helps people...

experiential.title: Experiential Values
experiential.description: What do you receive from the world? Think about relationships, nature, art, music, moments of beauty, love, and truth.
experiential.placeholder: e.g., The feeling of connection with my partner, sunsets, listening to live music...

attitudinal.title: Attitudinal Values
attitudinal.description: How do you respond to unavoidable suffering? Think of times you chose your attitude toward circumstances beyond your control.
attitudinal.placeholder: e.g., Choosing gratitude despite chronic illness, finding purpose in caregiving...

synthesis.title: Meaning Balance
synthesis.summaryText: You have {creative} creative, {experiential} experiential, and {attitudinal} attitudinal values.

intro.title: Socratic Self-Dialogue
intro.description: The answers are already within you. This exercise uses the Socratic method to help you discover them — a guided conversation that gently questions assumptions and illuminates what matters most.
intro.quote: What is to give light must endure burning.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

focus.title: What would you like to explore?

values.label: Values
values.description: I want to understand my values

decision.label: Decision
decision.description: I need to make a difficult decision

focus.customPlaceholder: Describe what you'd like to explore...

context.title: Add Context
context.description: You can add context to help guide the conversation — or skip ahead.
context.additionalPlaceholder: Anything else you'd like the guide to know...

dialogue.title: Dialogue
dialogue.maxReached: Maximum exchanges reached. Let's capture your insights.
dialogue.continueOrFinish: You can continue or finish the conversation.

insights.title: Insights
insights.insightLabel: What is the most important insight from this conversation?
insights.rememberLabel: What would you like to remember?
insights.journalCheckbox: Create a journal entry from these insights

intro.title: Mountain Range of Meaning
intro.description: Your life is a landscape of peaks and valleys. Each peak represents a moment of deep meaning; each valley, a time of struggle that shaped you. Together, they form your unique mountain range.
intro.peaksDescription: Moments when you felt most alive, fulfilled, or certain life had meaning.
intro.valleysDescription: Moments of deepest struggle, loss, or difficulty — and what they taught you.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

peaks.title: Your Peaks
peaks.description: Think of 3–7 moments when you felt most alive, most fulfilled, or most certain that life had meaning.

valleys.title: Your Valleys
valleys.description: Now think of 2–5 moments of deepest struggle, loss, or difficulty.
valleys.takeYourTime: Take your time with this. There's no rush.
valleys.growthPlaceholder: What did you learn, or how did you grow?

timeline.title: Your Mountain Range
timeline.description: Here is the landscape of your life. Click any point to see details.

themes.title: Discover Your Themes
themes.peaksCommonLabel: What do your peaks have in common?
themes.valleysCommonLabel: What do your valleys have in common?
themes.connectionLabel: How have your valleys led to future peaks?

future.title: Future Peaks
future.description: Based on the themes of your life, what future peaks would you like to create?
future.peakPlaceholder: A future peak you want to create...

intro.title: Paradoxical Intention Lab
intro.description: Viktor Frankl's paradoxical intention technique breaks anticipatory anxiety by deliberately wishing for the very thing you fear — with humor. By exaggerating the fear to absurdity, you create psychological distance from it.
intro.example1: A man who feared sweating was told to show everyone how much he could sweat: "I only sweated out a quart before, but now I'm going to sweat at least ten quarts!"
intro.example2: A bookkeeper who feared making errors was told to intentionally make as many errors as possible. His anxiety disappeared, and so did his errors.
intro.keyInsight: Key insight: Humor creates distance. When you laugh at a fear, it loses its grip.
intro.boundaryText: This exercise is designed for everyday anxieties and anticipatory fears. It is not suitable for deep trauma, severe phobias, or situations involving real danger. If your fears are related to trauma, please work with a professional.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

fears.title: Identify Your Fears
fears.description: What anxieties follow this pattern: the more you worry, the worse it gets?
fears.whatPlaceholder: e.g., Making a fool of myself in front of others...
fears.whatHappensLabel: What specifically do you fear will happen?
fears.whatHappensPlaceholder: e.g., I'll start stuttering and everyone will stare...

craft.title: Craft Paradoxical Intentions
craft.description: Now imagine you wanted this to happen. Wish for it with humor! The more absurd, the better.
craft.placeholder: e.g., I'll tremble so violently I register on the Richter scale!

practice.title: Practice Scripts
practice.description: Create a practice script for each fear. When the anxiety hits, what will you say to yourself?
practice.scriptPlaceholder: When I feel [the anxiety], I'll say to myself: "Great! Now's my chance to [paradoxical intention]! Let me really..."
practice.emotionLabel: How are you feeling now? Did humor help?

intro.title: Dereflection Practice
intro.description: When we fixate on a problem, it grows larger. Dereflection is Viktor Frankl's technique for redirecting attention away from the self and toward something meaningful in the world.
intro.quote: Being human always points, and is directed, to something, or someone, other than oneself.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

fixation.title: Your Fixation
fixation.whatLabel: What problem, symptom, or concern keeps consuming your attention?
fixation.whatPlaceholder: Describe what you keep thinking about...
fixation.energyLabel: How much mental energy does this consume daily?
fixation.impactLabel: How is this affecting your daily life?
fixation.impactPlaceholder: Describe the impact on your daily activities, relationships, mood...

redirections.title: Meaningful Redirections
redirections.description: What activities, people, or causes could you redirect your attention toward?
redirections.suggestionsSubtext: These life areas are going well and could nourish you:
redirections.activityPlaceholder: e.g., Volunteering at the community garden, calling my sister...

commit.title: Choose Your Commitments
commit.description: Choose 1-2 activities you'll commit to this week.
commit.whenPlaceholder: e.g., Tuesday and Thursday evenings after dinner
commit.pullbackLabel: What will you do when the fixation pulls you back?
commit.pullbackPlaceholder: e.g., I'll notice the thought, take a breath, and redirect to...

intro.title: Tragic Optimism
intro.description: Life includes suffering, guilt, and the awareness that our time is limited. Tragic optimism is the ability to say "yes" to life in spite of everything — to find meaning not despite these realities, but through engaging with them honestly.
intro.quote: In some way, suffering ceases to be suffering at the moment it finds a meaning, such as the meaning of a sacrifice.
intro.warningText: This exercise explores deep personal themes. If you're working through significant trauma, grief, or existential distress, consider doing this with a therapist or counselor who can provide professional support.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

focus.title: Which aspect of the tragic triad would you like to explore?

suffering.label: Suffering
suffering.description: Something I'm going through or have been through

guilt.label: Guilt
guilt.description: Something I feel responsible for or regret

finitude.label: Finitude
finitude.description: My awareness of limited time and what it means for how I live

write.title: Write Freely
write.description: Write freely about your experience. There's no right way to do this — just let your thoughts flow.

suffering.q1: What is the suffering you're carrying? Describe it as honestly as you can.
suffering.q2: Has this suffering changed you? If so, how?
suffering.q3: If this suffering could speak, what would it say it's trying to teach you?

guilt.q1: What do you feel guilty about? Be specific and honest with yourself.
guilt.q2: What does this guilt tell you about your values? What matters to you so much that its violation causes pain?
guilt.q3: If you could partially make amends or learn from this, what would that look like?

finitude.q1: When you think about the fact that your time is limited, what feelings arise?
finitude.q2: How does awareness of finitude change what matters to you?
finitude.q3: If you fully accepted that your time is limited, what would you do differently starting today?

dialogue.title: Dialogue
dialogue.reminder: Remember, working with a professional can deepen this exploration.
dialogue.completed: You've completed the dialogue. Let's capture your insights.

insights.title: Insights
insights.meaningLabel: What meaning, if any, have you found?
insights.carryLabel: What will you carry forward from this reflection?

intro.title: Attitudinal Shift
intro.quote: Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response. In our response lies our growth and our freedom.
intro.description: This exercise helps you transform the stories you tell yourself about unavoidable circumstances. You'll identify "because" statements — the narratives that keep you stuck — and find your choice point.
intro.exampleBefore: Because I lost my job, I'm a failure.
intro.exampleAfter: Although I lost my job, I choose to see this as an opportunity to find work that aligns with my values.
intro.exampleNote: The circumstances don't change. Your relationship to them does.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

statements.title: Your 'Because' Statements
statements.description: What stories do you tell yourself that start with "because"? These are the narratives where you feel trapped by circumstances.
statements.shadowTitle: Your shadow beliefs may help you identify patterns:
statements.placeholder: e.g., "Because I grew up poor, I'll never feel financially secure..."

shift.title: Find Your Choice Point
shift.helpButton: Help me find my choice point
shift.formatHint: Although [what happened], I choose to [your chosen response]

review.title: Your Shifts
review.surprisedLabel: Which reframe surprised you the most?
review.trueSelfLabel: Which one feels most true to who you want to be?

summary.title: Summary
summary.commitmentCheckbox: Create a commitment to practice a reframe this week

intro.title: Legacy Letter
intro.description: Viktor Frankl believed that awareness of our finite time can be our greatest motivator. This exercise invites you to write a letter from the perspective of having lived a full, meaningful life.
intro.secondaryDescription: What would you want to say? What would you want others to know? This isn't about perfection — it's about connecting with what truly matters to you.
intro.quote: Live as if you were living already for the second time and as if you had acted the first time as wrongly as you are about to act now.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

warmup.title: Warm Up
warmup.description: Before writing, reflect on a few of these prompts. Choose 2–3 that resonate.

prompts.p1: What do you want your children, family, or community to know about what mattered to you?
prompts.p2: What impact do you want to have had on the world?
prompts.p3: What wisdom have you earned through experience that you want to pass on?
prompts.p4: What would make you feel your time was well-spent?
prompts.p5: What do you want to be remembered for?

write.title: Your Letter
write.hint: Consider including: what you've created and contributed, the relationships that shaped you, the suffering you overcame and what it taught you, the values you lived by.

reflect.title: Reflect
reflect.description: Would you like to discuss your letter, or move on?
reflect.truthLabel: What felt most true in what you wrote?

safetyBanner.heading: Professional Guidance Recommended
safetyBanner.warningText: This exercise explores deep emotional material. While self-exploration is valuable, working with a therapist trained in IFS can provide safety and deeper healing — especially when strong emotions or early memories arise.
safetyBanner.checkboxLabel: I understand this is self-exploration, not therapy

intro.title: Parts Mapping
intro.description: In IFS, we all have different "parts" — inner voices, feelings, or impulses that each have a role in our life. This exercise helps you map them out.

managers.title: Managers
managers.description: Strategic parts that try to keep you safe by controlling your life (e.g., The Perfectionist, The Planner)

firefighters.title: Firefighters
firefighters.description: Reactive parts that extinguish pain when it breaks through (e.g., The Comfort Eater, The Netflix Binger)

exiles.title: Exiles
exiles.description: Vulnerable parts that carry old pain, often hidden by protectors (e.g., Little Me, The Lonely Child)

intro.quote: Every part has a positive intention, even the ones that cause us trouble.
intro.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now? (optional)

trailhead.title: Start with a Trailhead
trailhead.description: Think of a recent moment when you felt emotionally activated — more reactive than the situation seemed to warrant.
trailhead.bodyLocationLabel: Where did you feel it in your body?
trailhead.thoughtsLabel: What thoughts or inner voice showed up?
trailhead.thoughtsPlaceholder: e.g., 'You're not good enough', 'Something bad is going to happen'

identifyPart.title: Name This Part
identifyPart.description: Based on what you just described, let's identify the part that showed up.
identifyPart.nameLabel: What would you call this part?
identifyPart.namePlaceholder: e.g., The Worrier, The Controller, Little Me
identifyPart.protectionLabel: What is it trying to protect you from?
identifyPart.protectionPlaceholder: e.g., Being rejected, failing, feeling helpless
identifyPart.fearLabel: What is it afraid would happen if it stopped?
identifyPart.fearPlaceholder: e.g., Everything would fall apart, I'd be overwhelmed

instructions.tapTwo: Tap two parts to draw a connection

reflection.title: What Do You Notice?
reflection.description: Looking at your map, what stands out? Any surprises?
reflection.aiButton: Get AI Insights on Your Map
reflection.aiDisclaimer: This reflection is based on patterns in what you've shared. Your inner system is uniquely yours.

lifeAreas.title: Life Area Connections
lifeAreas.description: Which life areas do your parts affect most?
lifeAreas.emptyState: No life areas defined yet. You can add them later in the Life Areas section.

checkIn.title: Unblending Practice
checkIn.subtitle: Creating space between you and your parts — 2-3 minutes
checkIn.description: When we're "blended" with a part, we are the anxiety, the anger, the perfectionism. Unblending means stepping back into the calm observer seat — your Self.
checkIn.emotionLabel: How are you feeling right now?

awareness.title: Notice What's Here
awareness.description: Is there a strong feeling or reaction running the show right now? Something that feels like it IS you rather than something you're experiencing?

options.yes: Yes — something is really active right now

awareness.noResponse: That's great. Let's practice noticing anyway — it's a useful skill for when parts do activate.
awareness.partLabel: Does this feel like a part you know? (optional)

magicQuestion.title: The Magic Question
magicQuestion.question: How do you FEEL TOWARD this feeling or part?
magicQuestion.description: If you feel curious, compassionate, or calm toward it — that's your Self. If you feel annoyed, scared, or critical — that's another part reacting to the first one.

options.selfEnergy: I feel curious, open, or compassionate
options.reactive: I feel frustrated, scared, or judgmental

secondaryCheck.title: Another Part Arrived
secondaryCheck.description: That frustration, fear, or judgment is another part. Can you ask it to step back just a little, so you can get curious about the first part?
secondaryCheck.afterQuestion: How do you feel toward the original part now?

options.better: I feel more curious or compassionate now

secondaryCheck.partLabel: What would you call this second part? (optional)

steppingBack.title: Ask the Part to Step Back
steppingBack.description: Now, gently ask the part if it would be willing to step back just a little. Not go away — just give you some breathing room.

closing.title: How Much Space Do You Feel?

partSelect.title: Direct Access Dialogue
partSelect.description: Have a conversation directly with one of your parts. The AI will respond as the part would, based on what you know about it.
partSelect.emptyState: You need to create parts first. Go to Parts Mapping to identify and name your parts.
partSelect.label: Which part would you like to speak with?

selfCheck.title: Self-Energy Check
selfCheck.description: Before starting a dialogue, it's important to check that you're coming from a place of curiosity and openness — your Self — rather than from another part.
selfCheck.question: How do you feel toward {partName}?

options.selfEnergy: I feel curious, open, or compassionate
options.notQuite: Not quite — something else is reacting

selfCheck.continueButton: I feel more open now — let's continue

dialogue.title: Dialogue with {partName}
dialogue.emptyState: Start the conversation. Ask your part a question.
suggestedQuestions: What would happen if you stopped?
What do you need from me?

insightForm.title: Capture Insight
insightForm.placeholder: What did you notice or learn?

summary.title: Session Summary
summary.summaryPlaceholder: What stood out from this conversation?

job.label: Job discovered
job.placeholder: What does this part do for you?

fear.label: Fear discovered
fear.placeholder: What is this part most afraid of?

need.label: Need discovered
need.placeholder: What does this part need from you?

trigger.title: Trailhead Journal
trigger.subtitle: Use emotional triggers as doorways to understanding your parts
trigger.description: In IFS, a "trailhead" is any moment of emotional activation — a time when your reaction felt bigger than the situation warranted. These moments are invitations to discover which parts are at work.
trigger.questionPlaceholder: Think of a moment in the last day or two when you felt emotionally activated...
trigger.bodyLocationLabel: Where did you feel it in your body?

thoughts.title: Thoughts
thoughts.description: What thoughts or inner voices showed up in that moment?
thoughts.placeholder: e.g., 'I'm going to fail' or 'They don't care about me' or 'I need to fix this now'

sensations.title: Sensations
sensations.description: What physical sensations did you notice?
sensations.placeholder: e.g., tightness in chest, knot in stomach, heat in face, tension in shoulders

images.title: Images
images.description: Did any images, memories, or mental pictures appear?
images.optionalNote: (This is optional — not everyone experiences mental images)
images.placeholder: e.g., a memory from childhood, a mental picture of the worst case scenario

behaviors.title: Behaviors
behaviors.description: What did you do (or want to do) in response?
behaviors.placeholder: e.g., snapped at someone, withdrew, scrolled my phone, worked harder, ate comfort food

perception.title: Overall Perception
perception.description: Overall, did the moment feel more tense or more relaxed?

partLink.title: Which Part Was This?
partLink.description: Based on what you've described, does this feel like a part you already know?
partLink.unsureMessage: Not sure? That's fine — you can always link it to a part later.

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.reflectionLabel: What do you notice about when this part gets activated?
reflection.analysisDisclaimer: This analysis is based on patterns in your trailhead entries. Your inner experience is uniquely yours.
reflection.unlockAnalysis: Log {count} more trailhead(s) to unlock pattern analysis

selectProtector.title: Protector Appreciation
selectProtector.description: Build a relationship with a protector part by understanding its job, thanking it, and hearing its response.
selectProtector.emptyState: You need protector parts (managers or firefighters) to appreciate. Go to Parts Mapping to identify them.
selectProtector.label: Which protector would you like to appreciate?

understandJob.title: Understanding {partName}'s Job
understandJob.triggersLabel: What does this protector protect you from? When does it activate?
understandJob.triggersPlaceholder: Describe what triggers this part and what it protects you from...
understandJob.behaviorsLabel: How does it protect you? (select all that apply)
understandJob.workloadLabel: How hard is this protector working?

appreciationLetter.title: Write to {partName}
appreciationLetter.description: Write a letter of appreciation. This might be the first time this part has been thanked instead of fought.
appreciationLetter.placeholder:
Dear {partName},

I want you to know...

response.title: {partName}'s Response
response.promptQuestion: How would you like to hear back from {partName}?
response.aiOptionDescription: Based on what you know about this part
response.selfOptionDescription: Practice listening to what the part would say
response.selfPlaceholder: Write as {partName} would respond to your letter...

commitment.title: Commitment (Optional)
commitment.description: Is there something you'd like to offer {partName} to ease its workload?
chips: I'll try to notice you before reacting
I'll take a break when you activate
I'll journal when I feel you
I'll check in with you regularly

checkIn.title: Check-In Schedule (Optional)
checkIn.description: Would you like to regularly check in with {partName}?

safety.title: Exile Witnessing & Compassion
safety.description: This exercise guides you in gently approaching an exile — a vulnerable part that carries old pain or beliefs. You'll check in with protectors first, then simply be present with the exile and offer compassion.
safety.disclaimer: This is not unburdening — it's witnessing. You're creating a moment of connection, not trying to fix or heal. That deeper work is best done with a therapist.

protectorCheck.title: Getting Permission
protectorCheck.description: Before approaching an exile, we ask the protector parts that guard them. Select any protectors that might be involved.
protectorCheck.silentQuestion: Close your eyes. Silently ask: "Is it okay if I approach this exile?"

yes.label: Yes, go ahead
yes.description: The protectors feel safe enough to step aside

hesitant.label: Hesitant but okay
hesitant.description: They're nervous but willing to trust you

no.label: Not yet
no.description: They're not ready — the exile is too vulnerable right now

protectorCheck.blockingMessage: That's okay — the protectors aren't ready. Try the Protector Appreciation exercise first to build trust with them.

approach.title: Approaching the Exile
approach.description: Go slowly. Turn your attention inward and sense the vulnerable part.
approach.bodyLocationLabel: Where do you sense it in your body?
approach.feltAgeLabel: How old does this part feel?
approach.emotionLabel: What emotion is this part holding?

witness.title: Being Present
witness.description: Simply be with this part. You don't need to fix anything.
witness.messagePlaceholder: What does this part want you to know?
witness.beliefPlaceholder: What does it believe about you or the world?
witness.beliefNote: In IFS, these beliefs are called "burdens" — things the exile took on from painful experiences.

compassion.title: Offering Compassion
compassion.description: What would you like to say to this part?
compassion.settlePrompt: Take a moment to let your words settle.

closing.title: Closing
closing.description: How does the exile seem now?
closing.distressedWarning: Being seen sometimes brings up more feeling — that's normal and not a sign something went wrong. If this persists, consider sharing with a therapist who can provide deeper support.
closing.thankProtectors: Thank the protectors who allowed this. They showed courage too.

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.reflectionPlaceholder: What do you want to remember from this experience?

checkIn.title: Self-Energy Check-In
checkIn.subtitle: Rate your 8 C's — takes about 30 seconds

gap.title: Today's Growth Edge
gap.looksLike: It looks like {quality} is lower today (rated {rating}/5). That's okay — it often means a part is taking up that space.
gap.partQuestion: Can you sense which part might be affecting your {quality}?
gap.unsureMessage: Not sure? That's perfectly fine — just noticing the gap is valuable.

curiosity.title: The Curiosity Shift
curiosity.description: Pick something you're reacting to today. Ask yourself: "What if I got curious about this instead of frustrated?"
curiosity.placeholder: What happens when you approach it with curiosity?

compassion.title: Hand on Heart
compassion.description: Place your hand on your heart. Think of the part that's struggling. Silently say: "I see you. This is hard."

clarity.title: One True Thing
clarity.description: What's the simplest true thing about your situation right now?

courage.title: One Brave Thing
courage.description: What's one small brave thing you could do today?

creativity.title: Flip the Frame
creativity.description: What's a completely different way to look at today's challenge?

confidence.title: Recent Win
confidence.description: Name one thing you handled well recently.

connection.title: Reach Out
connection.description: Who could you reach out to today, even briefly?

summary.title: Check-In Complete
summary.trendDisclaimer: This review is based on patterns in your check-in data. Your experience is uniquely yours.
summary.unlockTrend: Complete {count} more check-in(s) to unlock trend analysis

partSelect.title: Parts Dialogue Journal
partSelect.description: Write a dialogue between your Self and a part. You write both sides of the conversation, practicing your own internal listening.
partSelect.label: Which part would you like to dialogue with?

intention.title: Set Your Intention
intention.description: What do you hope to explore in this dialogue with {partName}?
intention.placeholder: What do you want to explore?

dialogue.title: Dialogue with {partName}
dialogue.emptyState: Write as Self first, then switch to {partName}'s voice.
suggestedQuestions: What do you want me to know?

insights.title: Capture Insights
insights.description: Review your dialogue and tag any important moments.

selectPractice.title: Pick a Practice
selectPractice.subtitle: Quick micro-practices to stay connected with your inner system.

weatherReport.label: Parts Weather Report
weatherReport.description: Which parts are active? How intense?

gratitudeToPart.label: Gratitude to a Part
gratitudeToPart.description: Thank a part for what it did today

selfEnergyMoment.label: Self-Energy Moment
selfEnergyMoment.description: Which of the 8 C's do you need?

eveningReflection.label: Evening Reflection
eveningReflection.description: Review today's parts and your response

weatherReport.title: Parts Weather Report
weatherReport.description: Which parts are active right now?

gratitudeToPart.title: Gratitude to a Part
gratitudeToPart.description: Thank a part for what it did for you today.
gratitudeToPart.notePlaceholder: What did this part do for you today?

selfEnergyMoment.title: Self-Energy Moment
selfEnergyMoment.description: Which of the 8 C's do you most need right now?

calm.label: 60-Second Box Breathing
calm.prompt: Inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s. Repeat for one minute.
calm.placeholder: How do you feel after breathing?

curiosity.label: The Curiosity Shift
curiosity.prompt: Pick something you're reacting to. Ask: "What if I got curious instead of frustrated?"
curiosity.placeholder: What happens when you approach it with curiosity?

compassion.label: Hand on Heart
compassion.prompt: Place your hand on your heart. Think of the part that's struggling. Silently say: "I see you. This is hard."

clarity.label: One True Thing
clarity.prompt: What's the simplest true thing about your situation right now?

courage.label: One Brave Thing
courage.prompt: What's one small brave thing you could do today?

creativity.label: Flip the Frame
creativity.prompt: What's a completely different way to look at today's challenge?

confidence.label: Recent Win
confidence.prompt: Name one thing you handled well recently.

connection.label: Reach Out
connection.prompt: Who could you reach out to today, even briefly?

eveningReflection.title: Evening Reflection
eveningReflection.description: Review today's parts and your response.
eveningReflection.activePartsLabel: Which parts were most active today?
eveningReflection.appreciationPlaceholder: One thing you appreciate about today

summary.title: Check-In Complete
summary.moreForSummary: Complete {count} more check-in(s) this week for a weekly summary
summary.moreForSummarySingular: Complete {count} more check-in this week for a weekly summary
summary.moreForSummaryPlural: Complete {count} more check-ins this week for a weekly summary

prerequisites.title: Inner System Constellation
prerequisites.description: Parts form alliances, oppositions, and cascading chains. A Perfectionist and Procrastinator might look opposite but both protect the same wounded exile.
prerequisites.checkPassed: You have {count} parts mapped. Ready to explore their relationships.
prerequisites.checkFailed: You need at least 3 parts mapped. Try Parts Mapping first.

selectParts.title: Choose Your Focus
selectParts.description: Select 3-5 parts you'd like to explore the relationships between.

mapRelationships.title: How Do They Relate?
mapRelationships.notePlaceholder: When {partA} is active, what happens to {partB}?

polarizationDeepDive.title: Exploring Polarizations
polarizationDeepDive.description: Polarized parts are often the most revealing — they may both be protecting the same wound.
polarizationDeepDive.partBThinkPlaceholder: e.g., '{partA} is exhausting and never satisfied'
polarizationDeepDive.ifOneWon: What would happen if one of them "won"?
polarizationDeepDive.ifOneWonPlaceholder: What if one part completely took over?
polarizationDeepDive.commonProtection: What might they BOTH be protecting?
polarizationDeepDive.commonProtectionPlaceholder: Often, polarized parts protect the same exile from different angles.
polarizationDeepDive.commonProtectionHint: Hint: Polarized parts often guard the same vulnerable part from different angles.

polarizations.title: Polarizations
polarizations.description: Polarized parts look like opposites but often protect the same thing.
polarizations.questionLabel: What are {partA} and {partB} both trying to protect you from?

alliances.title: Alliances
alliances.description: Allied parts work together. They share a mission.
alliances.questionLabel: What is the shared mission of {partA} and {partB}?
alliances.questionPlaceholder: e.g., keeping you safe, avoiding conflict

cascades.title: Cascading Chains
cascades.description: Sometimes one part triggers another in a chain reaction.
cascades.instructions: If you notice a sequence (e.g., Critic triggers Procrastinator triggers Self-Blame), describe it here.
cascades.placeholder: Describe any chain reactions you notice...

reflection.title: System Insights
reflection.placeholder: Looking at your inner constellation, what patterns do you see?
reflection.aiDisclaimer: This analysis reflects patterns in what you've shared. A therapist trained in IFS can help explore these dynamics more deeply, especially where trauma may be involved.
